_subsystem_plan.subsystem_role: Tilting rotor nacelle assembly, 0-90deg tilt axis, 6309 bearing set, servo-driven worm gear, motor and ESC pocket
physics_paths: Load path: Rotor thrust load transfers through tilt axis to wing structure via bearing seats.
Flow path: Airflow around rotor nacelle influences wing lift and drag.
Thermal path: Heat from motor and ESC dissipates through nacelle housing to ambient air.
Motion path: Servo-driven worm gear rotates tilt axis, enabling nacelle tilt from 0 to 90 degrees.
Pressure path: Pressure differential across rotor blades generates lift force.
Service access path: Servo and ESC are accessible through access covers for maintenance and replacement.

manufacturing_strategy.datum: Primary datum is a flat surface at the bottom of the nacelle housing.
manufacturing_strategy.tolerance: +/- 0.2mm for FDM parts, +/- 0.1mm for CNC parts.
manufacturing_strategy.support: Support structures are removed after FDM printing.
manufacturing_strategy.inspection: Visual inspection for surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

generated_parts.label: Tilt-Axis Harness + Datum Service Bay
generated_parts.spec: Tiltrotor integration_service module with tilt-axis harness_channel, slack_loop, interface_datum, service_hatch, connector keying, and nacelle-to-wing load-path inspection access

requirements: integration - role: Nacelle interface datums, harness routing through tilt axis, hatches, connection types and load-path closure
interface_datum, harness_channel, service_hatch, tilt_axis, service_window, harness_path
Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown
Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels
Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover
Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges
Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating
Primary locating faces, rail guides, axis rings, spar sockets, or mating pads are visible and labeled
Critical slip, snap, pin, hinge, and slide interfaces state plausible clearances
Each subsystem has at least three adjacent interfaces or datums
Fits are named with standard intent: clearance fit for sliding, transition for locating, interference/press only where justified, with a numeric allowance
Holes, slots, and pockets match real catalog hardware sizes (M3/M4 clearance holes, standard bearing OD, common pin diameters)

Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance

Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified
One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack
Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types

Compared to the previous version the design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints
The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer
P0 internal_feature_targets appear as part_tree children and geometry_ops ids or targets
Most geometry_ops include args.at coordinates distributed across the local subsystem volume
Uses drill, pocket, boss, channel, pattern_polar, fillet, chamfer — not just boxes
Every part_tree child has 4+ dedicated geometry_ops (body+subtract+interface+finish)
Uses seed-characteristic ops: chamfer, pocket, drill, subtract. Subtractive features (drill/pocket/channel) and finishing (fillet/chamfer) both present
Dimensions match tiltrotor scale: 200-1500mm wingspan, 50-300mm fuselage dia. Wall thickness, hole diameters, fillet radii are physically plausible
cad_brief.mass_est_g must be a numeric grams value, and verify must include a mass/budget check
Include at least three adjacent-module interfaces or mounting datums and at least three negative/service features such as holes, pockets, ducts, channels, ports, or access cuts
Name exact neighboring subsystem labels when they mate, and choose connection_type + DOF + clearance_mm + required_feature so the neighbor can declare the same joint family back
Budget rule: cad_brief.mass_est_g must be a numeric grams value, and verify must include a mass/budget check
Critical joints need visible insert/washer/locking/preload evidence and torque_Nm or preload intent
Training-quality rule: make part_tree children and geometry_ops mutually traceable by id/name
Verify must include a boundary_conditions object with fixture_points, thermal_zones, and pressure_faces
Assembly sequence rule: cad_brief must include assembly_sequence and disassembly_sequence for field-serviceable parts
For each adjacent_interface in the subsystem plan, include stack_up_mm and fit_class
All dimensions must be physically plausible for the subsystem scale
Wall thickness, hole diameters, fillet radii must match the stated material and process (FDM min wall 1.2mm, SLA 0.5mm, CNC 0.8mm)
No zero-dimension or placeholder values

verify.rule: All required fields are present.
mass_est_g is a numeric value.
P0 internal_feature_targets appear as part_tree children and geometry_ops ids or targets.
Each interface has a named target part, connection type, DOF, clearance, and required feature.
Critical joints have visible locking or preload evidence.
Boundary conditions are defined for downstream CFD/FEA.

risk.desc: Potential for harness strain relief failure due to sharp edges around cable exits.
risk.mit: Add strain relief geometry (clamps, grommets) around cable exits.

risk.desc: Service hatch may not provide sufficient access for all maintenance tasks.
risk.mit: Increase size of service hatch or add additional access points.

risk.desc: Environmental barrier may not be sufficient to protect against salt fog in maritime environments.
risk.mit: Use a more resistant material or add additional protective coatings.

risk.desc: Potential for thermal expansion issues due to large temperature range (-20 to +50C).
risk.mit: Include thermal expansion joints or use materials with similar thermal expansion coefficients.

risk.desc: Connector keying may be insufficient to prevent mis-mating under field conditions.
risk.mit: Add additional keying or labeling to connectors.
